Its AI cloud service revenue reached $17.3 million (9% of total revenue), while the Horizon Phase 1–4 GPU expansion project (up to 200 megawatts) is under construction. CLSK (CleanSpark) BTC Produced: 1,821 BTC Total Cost: $118,932/BTC Cash Cost (Pre-tax): $71,188/BTC CleanSpark demonstrates exceptional operational discipline. Its S&A (Selling and Administrative Expenses) of $17,848/BTC and SBC (Share-Based Compensation Expenses) of $6,662/BTC are among the lowest in its industry. A 100% allocation ratio (pure mining, no hosting/HPC revenue) simplifies cost analysis. Electricity costs were $52,463/BTC, up from $44,679 in the second quarter, reflecting increased mining difficulty. With an installed capacity of approximately 50 EH/s, the cluster efficiency of approximately 16 W/T remains industry-leading. Depreciation and amortization (D&A) was $58,381/BTC, roughly in line with peers. Interest expense was extremely low ($830/BTC), reflecting its low-leverage balance sheet. New CEO Matt Schultz (who will succeed Zach Bradford in August 2025) stated that the hashrate could potentially climb to approximately 60 EH/s if market conditions allow. The company is exploring diversification of equipment suppliers to reduce its reliance on Bitmain. No concrete AI/HPC plans have been announced yet, but management has hinted at the possibility of monetizing data center assets located near the metropolitan area (Georgia facility). Note: CLSK's fiscal year ends September 30, meaning the current data pertains to the first quarter of its fiscal year 2026. RIOT (Riot Platforms) BTC Produced: 1,324 BTC Total Cost: $170,366/BTC Cash Cost (Pre-tax): $102,538/BTC Riot produced 1,324 BTC with an average deployment hashrate of 31.5 EH/s. The $9.9 million ERCOT demand response credit in the fourth quarter (totaling $56.7 million for the full fiscal year) significantly benefited its electricity costs of $49,196/BTC, effectively offsetting total electricity costs. Selling and administrative expenses (SG&A) were among the highest in the industry at $31,534/BTC, reflecting corporate administrative costs and development expenditures for the 1 GW Corsicana project. Share-based compensation expenses (SBC) reached a high level of $21,586/BTC. Depreciation and amortization (D&A) were $66,900/BTC, reflecting continued investment in mining equipment. As of December 31, the company held 17,722 BTC (worth over $1.5 billion at closing prices). Riot's strategic focus is on the Corsicana project, with 600 megawatts designated for AI workloads. While this represents a significant long-term opportunity, Q4 revenue was still largely driven by mining operations. The total site capacity of 1 gigawatt makes Riot one of the largest single-site facility operators in North America. CORZ (Core Scientific) BTC Production: 421 BTC Total Cost: $168,693/BTC Cash Cost: $110,282/BTC Q4 was a milestone for CORZ's transition to AI/HPC. Hosting revenue reached $31.3 million (39% of total revenue, up from $8.5 million in Q4 2024). Proprietary mining revenue decreased year-over-year to $42.2 million from $79.9 million, due to a deliberate shift of production capacity to the HPC sector. Lower BTC production (421 BTC) boosted metrics per BTC. Sales and administrative expenses (SG&A) were $47,510/BTC and share-based compensation expenses (SBC) were $35,506/BTC, both among the highest in the industry, reflecting corporate administrative costs and the costs of the failed CoreWeave merger. A fleet efficiency ratio of approximately 24.7 W/T lagged behind peers (15–18 W/T), resulting in electricity costs as high as $66,720/BTC. While the failed CoreWeave merger (October 30, 2025) has introduced uncertainty, execution continues: approximately 350 MW is energized, and approximately 200 MW is billed, with a target of 590 MW fully operational by early 2027 (contract value of $10.2 billion over 12 years). Due to improper capitalization of assets committed to be dismantled during the HPC conversion, the company made a significant restatement of its financial data for 2024–2025, resulting in a change of auditor to KPMG and the assessment that internal controls were ineffective. Depreciation and amortization (D&A) was $17,701/BTC, the lowest among its peers, reflecting in part the impairment of assets following the restatement. WULF (TeraWulf)

BTC Production: 262

Combined Cost: $471,841/BTC

Cash Cost: $384,517/BTC

Important Note: WULF's cost per BTC is not comparable to pure mining peers.

The company has fundamentally transformed into an AI/HPC infrastructure enterprise, maintaining only a shrinking mining operation. The 262 BTC mined this quarter were generated in conjunction with $9.7 million in HPC rental revenue.

The company has fundamentally transformed into an AI/HPC infrastructure enterprise, maintaining only a shrinking mining operation.

Fourth-quarter mining revenue declined 40% quarter-over-quarter (QoQ) to $26.1 million. HPC rental revenue increased 35% quarter-over-quarter to $9.7 million (representing 27% of total fourth-quarter revenue). Total revenue for fiscal year 2025 is $168.5 million, of which $16.9 million is contributed by the HPC business. The extremely high overall costs reflect the following factors: interest at $144,974/BTC (total debt of $5.7 billion: including $2.5 billion in convertible notes and $3.2 billion in senior secured notes from WULF Compute); selling and administrative expenses (SG&A) of $167,221/BTC (primarily due to workforce expansion and milestone compensation); and depreciation and amortization (D&A) of $77,217/BTC (due to new HPC infrastructure). By the end of 2025, the company's cash reserves reached $3.7 billion (up from $274 million previously), reflecting substantial capital formation. It currently has 522 megawatts of contracted capacity, involving long-term customer contracts worth $12.8 billion. CIFR (Cipher Digital)

BTC Production: 591

Comprehensive Cost: $231,980/BTC

Cash Cost: $103,516/BTC

CIFR's comprehensive cost is the second highest (excluding WULF), primarily driven by depreciation and amortization (D&A) of up to $87,768/BTC, based on a 3-year useful life assumption adopted in 2024, and interest expense of $56,445/BTC. ...">CIFR (Cipher Digital)

BTC Production: 591

Comprehensive Cost: $231,980/BTC

Cash Cost: $103,516/BTC

CIFR's comprehensive cost is the second highest (excluding WULF), primarily driven by depreciation and amortization (D&A) of up to $87,768/BTC, based on a 3-year useful life assumption adopted in 2024, and interest expense of $56,445/BTC.

CIFR (Cipher Digital)

Cipher Digital (Cipher Digital)

HUT (Hut 8 Corp.)

BTC Production: 719

Comprehensive Cost: $160,402/BTC

Cash Cost: $50,332/BTC

Hut 8's apparent comprehensive cost appears competitive, but it requires careful interpretation due to several one-off items.

Its equity compensation expense (SBC) is a high $48,527/BTC, the highest among its peers, primarily driven by the equity awards (2.3 million restricted stock units (RSUs) and performance stock units (PSUs)) to be awarded to the CEO and CSO in November 2025.

Fourth-quarter SBC was $39.7 million, compared to only $18.1 million for the first nine months—a 2.2-fold increase. Normalizing SBC would significantly reduce overall costs. Due to a $17.8 million Canadian Harmonized Sales Tax (HST) refund in December 2025, its general and administrative expenses (G&A, excluding SBC) of $7,413/BTC appear artificially low. Normalized G&A should be closer to approximately $30,000/BTC. The depreciation and amortization (D&A) of $48,621/BTC is a consolidated figure; mining-related D&A is actually lower (due to approximately 74% of plant and equipment (PP&E) being mining-related). The interest expense of $6,840/BTC reflects its total debt of approximately $411 million (including TZRC debt at 15.25%, Coinbase debt at 9%, and Coatue convertible bonds at 8%). Thanks to Bitmain mining rigs at its Vega site (with a hashrate of 14.86 EH/s), its BTC production climbed to 719 from 578 in Q3. The company currently holds 15,679 BTC (worth approximately $1.37 billion). Its complex business structure (including four business units, the ABTC subsidiary, and inter-company offsets) makes clear cost attribution extremely challenging. The $78.2 million income tax gain (reversal of deferred income tax) in Q4 has been excluded from the calculation. BTDR (Bitdeer Technologies Group) BTC Produced: 1,673 BTC Overall Cost: $118,188/BTC Cash Cost: $87,144/BTC Bitdeer's overall cost is highly competitive among its peers, although this reflects, to some extent, International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) practices and multi-segment revenue (SEALMINER mining machine sales revenue $23.4 million, HPC/AI revenue $2.3 million). Average electricity cost rose to $46/MWh from $43/MWh in Q3. The most notable issue was the depreciation policy change in the fourth quarter: management shortened the lifespan of mining rigs, causing depreciation and amortization (D&A) in cost of revenue (CoR) for self-operated mining to more than double sequentially (from $31.2 million to $63.9 million), despite a roughly 60% increase in hashrate. Gross margin for self-operated mining plummeted to 3.6% from 27.7% in the third quarter. This was purely an accounting consequence, not a deterioration in operating conditions. Under IFRS reporting practices, D&A and SBC are bundled in cost of revenue (CoR), complicating comparisons with peers using US GAAP. Interest expense of $16,306/BTC reflects approximately $1 billion in convertible notes and related-party borrowings. BTDR's proprietary ASIC chip strategy (the SEALMINER A2 with an energy efficiency ratio of 16.5 W/T, and the soon-to-be-mass-produced A3 with an energy efficiency ratio of 9.7 W/T) is a significant competitive advantage, greatly reducing capital expenditure per TH of hashrate (capex/TH) compared to purchasing mining machines from Bitmain.

HIVE (HIVE Digital Technologies)

BTC Production: 884

Comprehensive Cost: $144,321/BTC

Cash Cost: $75,274/BTC

HIVE mined 884 BTC in the fourth quarter (its third fiscal quarter ending December 31), with a significant increase in production driven by the expansion of its Paraguayan operations. The fleet's energy efficiency ratio improved from 21 W/T to 18.5 W/T. The electricity cost of $65,368/BTC is the highest among its peers (excluding WULF), driven by a forward-looking accounting change: HIVE capitalized $41.3 million of non-refundable Paraguayan Value Added Tax (VAT) in Plant and Equipment (PP&E) and expensed $5.5 million of electricity VAT through operating expenses (opex). This accounting treatment increased both its D&A and electricity costs compared to its peers. Its selling and administrative expenses (SG&A) of $9,054/BTC are among the lowest. The share-based compensation expense (SBC) of $7,501/BTC is moderate (corresponding to an RSU paid out at C$7.30 in October 2025). With interest expenses at just $320/BTC, the lowest among its peers, HIVE's total debt of only $13.8 million provides a significant structural advantage. During the quarter, a 100 MW Valenzuela facility became operational; HIVE now holds a total of 300 MW of ANDE power purchase agreements in Paraguay. The company faces approximately $79.2 million in contingent VAT liabilities (stemming from an assessment of its Bikupa subsidiary by the Swedish Tax Authority, currently under appeal). Its use of 2,079 BTC with repurchase options to pay for equipment deposits is an unusual capital management strategy. BITF (Bitfarms) will be updated after Bitfarms releases its Q4 earnings report. VI. Mining Company Stock Performance and Valuation In the fourth quarter, the valuation premium for AI/HPC continued to widen. Currently, the enterprise value to future twelve-month sales (EV/NTM sales) ratio for mining companies that have secured HPC contracts has reached 12.3 times, while the ratio for pure mining companies is only 5.9 times. The decline in BTC price in the fourth quarter (a 31% pullback from its all-time high) created a double headwind: it not only reduced mining revenue but also significantly reduced the value of BTC holdings in mining companies' coffers. The valuation discount experienced by CORZ after the failed merger (possibly due to hedge fund liquidation) contrasts sharply with the valuation premium enjoyed by WULF, CIFR, and HUT. Currently, short interest across the entire sector is high; as of this writing, short interest in MARA accounts for approximately 30% of its outstanding shares. The industry has fundamentally diverged into "infrastructure companies" (such as WULF, CORZ, CIFR, and HUT) and "mining companies" (such as MARA, CLSK, RIOT, and HIVE). Whether these AI-driven high valuation multiples are reasonable ultimately depends on the companies' execution capabilities: not all announced agreements translate into operational infrastructure, and the capital requirements behind them remain extremely large.

VII. First Quarter of 2026 and Future Outlook

1. The recovery of hashrate prices depends on the BTC price: With the BTC price around $70,000 and the hashrate price around $30/PH/day, many mid-generation mining rigs are already at or below the break-even point. If the price continues to be below $70,000, it could trigger a larger-scale "miner capitulation," but this, in turn, would benefit the survivors by reducing mining difficulty and the overall network hashrate.

2. Deployment of Next-Generation Hardware: Bitmain's S23 series and SEALMINER A3 (both with energy efficiency ratios below 10 J/TH) are expected to achieve large-scale deployment in the first half of 2026, which will further widen the energy efficiency gap and accelerate the replacement cycle of mining rigs. 3. Inflection Point for AI/HPC Revenue: CORZ aims to fully deliver its 590 MW CoreWeave project by early 2027. WULF's Lake Mariner site expansion continues. The market will closely watch whether contracted revenue can be converted into actual billing and whether profit margins can reach the target of over 85%. 4. Diverging leverage ratios fuel M&A activity: Mining companies with healthy balance sheets and strong liquidity (such as HIVE and CLSK) are likely to be acquirers; however, even CLSK has already taken on a substantial amount of convertible debt ($1.15 billion, zero interest) to finance its transformation into AI infrastructure. 5. Shifting geographic distribution and regulatory environment: The US continues to expand its market share. Paraguay and Ethiopia are emerging as major mining hubs. Enforcement actions in Xinjiang, China, may prompt a shift of computing power overseas. Texas SB 6 (signed in June 2025) introduces new requirements for the power load of large-scale mining and data center connections to ERCOT, including mandatory remote power-off capabilities. 6. Industry consolidation: We expect more M&A activity in 2026. The energy efficiency gap between top-tier rigs (energy efficiency ratio of approximately 15 W/T) and lagging rigs (energy efficiency ratio of approximately 25 W/T or higher) is large enough that directly acquiring high-efficiency capacity may be less costly than upgrading aging operational facilities. Appendix: Methodology Denominator: BTC produced by self-mining this quarter. Allocation Ratio: Self-mining revenue / Total revenue. This ratio applies to selling and administrative expenses (SG&A), depreciation and amortization (D&A), share-based compensation expenses (SBC), interest, and taxes.

All-In Cost per BTC = Electricity Cost (after deducting curtailment compensation) + SG&A (excluding SBC) + D&A + Net Interest + Income Tax + SBC — — Where applicable, all items are amortized proportionally to mining revenue.

Cash Cost per BTC = Revenue Cost (excluding D&A) + SG&A (excluding SBC) + Net Interest + Income Tax — — All items are amortized proportionally.

Electricity Cost: Deducted for curtailment/demand response credits. This cost analysis excludes asset impairment, fair value revaluation, and non-operating items (e.g., BTC revaluation gains/losses, fair value changes of derivatives, debt conversion inducement fees).

Units of measurement: Unless otherwise stated, all figures are in thousands of US dollars. Financial data not denominated in US dollars has been translated using the quarterly average exchange rate.

data.title: An in-depth investigation into the authenticity of trading volumes and market share of cryptocurrency exchanges.
data.description:
Author: danny Source: X,@agintender

The trading volume of crypto exchanges may be the biggest lie in the industry. This article uses on-chain data to reverse-engineer the real monthly trading volume of the top 8 centralized exchanges (CEXs), and the conclusion is counterintuitive. Are the market shares of Binance, OKX, and Bitget underestimated?

Volume manipulation is almost cost-free—market makers have no fees, API matching doesn't require on-chain settlement, and VIP rebates can even make you "earn more the more you trade." As a result, if you open any exchange ranking website, half of the numbers you see may be ghost trades.

But decentralized exchanges on-chain are different. Every transaction requires real margin, real gas fees, and is completely transparent. This gives us a benchmark.

We collected official reserve data and 30-day trading volume data from eight major centralized exchanges (CEXs): Binance, OKX, Bybit, Bitget, Gate.io, MEXC, KuCoin, and HTX, as well as Hyperliquid. Using Hyperliquid's on-chain data as a "benchmark," we deduced the true extent of each exchange's reported monthly trading volume. The conclusions are not surprising: of the $361 billion in trading volume reported by one exchange in the past 30 days, more than half is estimated to be inflated; Binance's true market share is not 39%, but likely closer to 46%; and the entire industry may have over $300 billion in fabricated monthly trading volume.

30-day daily average

... Vol/Reserve Ratio Ranking

Data Period: February-March 2026 (30 days) | Source: Official Proof of Reserves of various exchanges, DefiLlama, CoinGlass, Newhedge

Note: This article only uses a sample of these eight exchanges for research and does not represent the entire market share. The actual conclusions will definitely have errors!

I. Benchmark: Why is Hyperliquid the benchmark?

To determine whether the trading volume of a CEX is real, we first need a "benchmark". We choose Hyperliquid.

Hyperliquid is currently the largest on-chain perpetual contract DEX, with almost all of its $4.88 billion TVL in USDC.

The cost of fabricating trading volume is high: each transaction requires locking USDC margin, incurring real funding costs; its OI (On-the-Book) activity is also among the highest; gas and transaction fees are unavoidable; and it's completely transparent on-chain, with every transaction auditable. After all, they are all exchanges, operating in the same field, and trading similar assets, so theoretically, user habits and capital utilization efficiency should be similar. However, Hyperliquid, as a "contract trading-focused" exchange, should have a higher capital utilization rate than CEXs, hence Hyperliquid is used as the benchmark. Our core method is to calculate each exchange's "average daily trading volume/reserve ratio": [See: https://x.com/agintender/status/1992906704925884898?s=20](https://x.com/agintender/status/1992906704925884898?s=20) Formula = 30-day total trading volume ÷ 30 ÷ Core reserve assets (BTC+ETH+USDT+USDC) Hyperliquid's total contract trading volume over the past 30 days was $206.8 billion (precise data from the DefiLlama API), averaging $6.89 billion per day, corresponding to $4.88 billion TVL, with a daily average ratio of 1.44x.

This 1.44x This represents the ceiling for "honest trading." Considering that CEXs have more diverse uses for funds than pure contract DEXs (including spot trading, wealth management, lending, etc.), we give CEXs a 15% tolerance range, widening the threshold to 1.66x.

II. Data Foundation: Calibrating Reserve Proofs for Each CEX

Before inferring trading volume, the reserve data must be accurate—it is the denominator of the formula.

III. 30-Day Trading Volume Overview

Data Source

  • Spot Monthly Trading Volume: Newhedge (March 2026 Data)

  • Derivatives Monthly Trading Volume: CoinGlass Multiple Snapshots Average × 30 (Marked as Estimated)

  • Hyperliquid: DefiLlama API 30-Day Data

IV. Contract Trading Volume Breakdown

Contracts are CEX...

The absolute main force of trading volume, the authenticity of which directly determines the credibility of the total volume.

Contract vs Spot Market Two-Dimensional Anomaly Detection (Bubble Chart)

VI. Inverse Deduction of Actual Trading Volume

For exchanges whose daily average total ratio exceeds the tolerance threshold (1.66x), we will consider the excess portion as "suspicious trading volume":

Reserve Rate Heat Table

VIII. Industry Impact

MEXC ~$201 billion + Gate.io ~$54 billion + KuCoin ~$40 billion = $295 billion/month in suspicious trading volume. If hundreds of small and medium-sized exchanges are included, the industry's overall monthly fake trading volume may be in the range of $350-450 billion.

For ordinary users: Don't be misled by "trading volume rankings". An exchange with a monthly trading volume of $361 billion may only have a real trading volume of $160 billion. When choosing an exchange, look at the vol/reserve ratio, not the absolute trading volume.

For project teams: When choosing an exchange for listing, look at the actual order book depth, not the reported trading volume.

More than half of the monthly report figures may be non-existent transactions. For regulators: The Hyperliquid benchmark provides a reproducible quantitative detection tool. As on-chain exchange data accumulates, 30-day and 90-day analyses will make fraud increasingly difficult to detect. IX. Methodology and Limitations The Hyperliquid benchmark may be too high. Pure contract platforms use all user funds for trading, making their capital efficiency naturally higher than CEXs. 1.44x may already be a high benchmark for CEXs, meaning our estimate may be conservative. Excess reserves blur the lines. Bitget (BTC 237%), MEXC (BTC 270%), and Gate.io (BTC 147%)'s own funds inflate the ratio but do not equate to wash trading.

The 30-day derivatives volume is an estimate. CEX's 30-day derivatives trading volume is based on multiple snapshots from CoinGlass, not precise historical data. Spot 30-day data comes from Newhedge's precise monthly data.

Reserve data comes from self-disclosure. If reserves were inflated, our ratio would be lower, and suspicious trading volume would be underestimated.

Conclusion: Four numbers

0.44x — Binance's 30-day average daily trading volume/reserve ratio. Almost a "proof of innocence." When the industry is rife with false prosperity, Binance is the one that doesn't need inflated volume to maintain its leading position.

56% — The possible inflated percentage in MEXC's reported monthly trading volume.

56% — The percentage of inflated volume in MEXC's reported monthly trading volume.

$361 billion in monthly trading volume to $3.2 billion in reserves, a daily ratio of 3.76x, is 2.6 times the benchmark. Approximately $201 billion in monthly trading volume is likely fictitious. 46% – Binance's adjusted true monthly market share. While it appears to be only 39%, after stripping away competitors' inflated figures, Binance's true dominance is close to half. Some exchanges claiming to be catching up with Binance are merely chasing their own shadow. 76% – The combined true monthly market share of the top three exchanges, adjusted. This market is more oligopolistic than retail investors imagine – fictitious trading volume has been masking this fact. Postscript: We initially used DefiLlama's on-chain tracking data, but after cross-validating the official Proof of Reserves of various exchanges, we found significant discrepancies—DefiLlama generally underestimated reserves, with discrepancies ranging from 5% to 240%.
